title: Run the load test and read results

blocks.content: In this milestone, you execute `baseline.js` and focus on the summary lines that matter for a baseline: duration, failures, checks, and throughput.
You'll confirm the test completes cleanly before you treat any numbers as a benchmark.

Open your terminal and run:

```bash
k6 run baseline.js
```

k6 prints live progress while the scenario executes.

When the test completes, review **`http_req_duration`**. The summary lists aggregations similar to:

```text
http_req_duration..: avg=180ms  min=95ms
                     med=165ms  max=850ms
                     p(90)=280ms  p(95)=350ms
```

- `avg` is easy to skew with outliers.
- `p(90)` and `p(95)` describe most users' experience.
- If `max` dwarfs `p(95)`, a small slice of requests is much slower.

Review **`http_req_failed`** for error rate:

```text
http_req_failed................: 0.00%   0 out of 1200
```

For a healthy baseline this should stay at or near zero.

Review **`checks`** under **TOTAL RESULTS** on newer k6 versions:

```text
checks_total.......: 2400    10.00/s
checks_succeeded...: 100.00% 2400 out of 2400
checks_failed......: 0.00%   0 out of 2400

✓ status is 200
✓ response body is not empty
```

100% `checks_succeeded` means every response satisfied your predicates.

Review **`iterations`** for throughput:

```text
iterations.....................: 1200    10.00/s
```

The per-second figure approximates completed iterations per second during the run.

Confirm the run looks stable: checks succeed, errors are negligible, and latency percentiles are not wildly noisy. Investigate anomalies before recording a baseline.

Percentiles are more reliable than averages for baselines. An average of 200 ms could hide a few 2 s responses; p95 surfaces that risk.
You can now read the summary well enough to pick baseline numbers in the next milestone.
In the next milestone, you extract concrete baseline values from these metrics.
